full_name: MDCG 2019-16 rev.1 - Guidance on Cybersecurity for Medical Devices

articles.title: Scope and Purpose
articles.text: This document provides guidance on how to fulfil all relevant essential requirements of Annex I of Regulation (EU) 2017/745 (MDR) and Regulation (EU) 2017/746 (IVDR) with regard to cybersecurity. It covers the pre-market aspects of cybersecurity as well as the post-market obligations of the manufacturer. The guidance is aimed at medical device manufacturers, notified bodies and competent authorities. This document should be read in conjunction with the MDR and IVDR regulations and relevant harmonised standards, in particular EN IEC 81001-5-1:2022 and IEC 62443 series.

articles.title: Applicable Regulatory Requirements
articles.text:
The General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPRs) set out in Annex I to the MDR and IVDR include requirements related to cybersecurity, although the term cybersecurity is not explicitly used. The following GSPRs are particularly relevant:

- GSPR 17.2 (MDR) / GSPR 16.2 (IVDR): Devices that incorporate electronic programmable systems, including software, or software that are devices in themselves, shall be designed to ensure repeatability, reliability and performance in line with their intended use. In the event of a single fault condition, appropriate means shall be adopted to eliminate or reduce as far as possible consequent risks or impairment of performance.

- GSPR 17.4 (MDR) / GSPR 16.4 (IVDR): For devices that incorporate software or for software that are devices in themselves, the software shall be developed and manufactured in accordance with the state of the art taking into account the principles of development life cycle, risk management, including information security, verification and validation.

- GSPR 14.2 (MDR/IVDR): Devices shall be designed and manufactured in such a way as to remove or reduce as far as possible risks associated with the possible negative interaction between software and the IT environment within which it operates and interacts.

- GSPR 14.5 (MDR/IVDR): The label shall bear the warnings or precautions to be taken and any limitations in use of the device, including information related to IT security measures.

- GSPR 23.4 (MDR): Devices that are intended to be operated together with other devices or products shall be designed and manufactured in such a way that interoperability and compatibility are reliable and safe.

articles.title: Secure Design and Development (Pre-market)
articles.text:
Manufacturers shall implement a Secure Product Development Framework (SPDF) throughout the entire lifecycle of the device. This includes:

3.1 Security Risk Management: The manufacturer shall implement and document a cybersecurity risk management process that is integrated with the overall device risk management process (ISO 14971). This includes identifying cybersecurity threats and vulnerabilities, assessing associated risks to patients and users, implementing risk control measures, and evaluating the residual risk.

3.2 Threat Modeling: The manufacturer shall perform threat modeling to systematically identify and evaluate cybersecurity threats. The threat model should consider: the device architecture and interfaces, data flows (especially personal and health data), trust boundaries, potential threat agents and attack vectors, and the clinical context of use.

3.3 Security Requirements: Based on the risk assessment and threat model, the manufacturer shall define cybersecurity requirements covering: authentication and access control, data encryption (at rest and in transit), audit logging and monitoring, input validation, secure update mechanisms, and secure default configuration.

3.4 Secure Development Practices: The manufacturer shall follow secure coding practices including static and dynamic code analysis, code review, dependency management, and Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generation.

3.5 Security Testing: The manufacturer shall conduct cybersecurity testing including vulnerability scanning, penetration testing, fuzz testing, and verification of security controls.

3.6 Software Bill of Materials (SBOM): The manufacturer shall generate and maintain an SBOM listing all software components, including open-source and third-party libraries, their versions, known vulnerabilities, and support status. The SBOM shall be made available to notified bodies and competent authorities upon request.

articles.title: Technical Documentation Requirements
articles.text:
The technical documentation submitted for conformity assessment shall include:

4.1 Security Risk Management File: Documentation of the cybersecurity risk management process, including threat modeling results, risk assessment, risk control measures implemented, and residual risk evaluation.

4.2 Design Specifications: Description of security architecture, security controls implemented, cryptographic mechanisms used, authentication and authorization mechanisms, and data protection measures.

4.3 Verification and Validation: Results of security testing, including vulnerability assessments, penetration test reports, and verification that security requirements have been met.

4.4 SBOM: Complete Software Bill of Materials including all software components, versions, licenses, and known vulnerability status.

4.5 Secure Update Plan: Description of the mechanism for providing security patches and updates, including the process for evaluating and prioritizing vulnerabilities, testing updates before release, and communicating updates to users.

4.6 Instructions for Use (IFU): Security-relevant information for users, including network configuration requirements, recommended security settings, user account management procedures, and instructions for applying security updates.

articles.title: Post-market Cybersecurity Obligations
articles.text:
After placing the device on the market, the manufacturer shall:

5.1 Vulnerability Monitoring: Continuously monitor for newly discovered vulnerabilities affecting the device or its components, including monitoring CVE databases, vendor security advisories, and cybersecurity intelligence sources.

5.2 Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (CVD): Establish and maintain a coordinated vulnerability disclosure policy, providing a secure channel for security researchers to report vulnerabilities. The manufacturer shall acknowledge receipt, evaluate the reported vulnerability, and provide timely remediation.

5.3 Security Patch Management: Develop and deploy security patches in a timely manner. Critical vulnerabilities shall be addressed with urgency. Patches shall be tested for compatibility and safety before deployment.

5.4 Field Safety Corrective Actions (FSCA): When a cybersecurity vulnerability poses an unacceptable residual risk, the manufacturer shall initiate a field safety corrective action in accordance with MDR Article 83 or IVDR Article 78. This may include software updates, configuration changes, or device recall.

5.5 Post-market Surveillance: Integrate cybersecurity monitoring into the post-market surveillance system (MDR Article 83). The Post-Market Surveillance Plan (PMSP) and Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR) shall address cybersecurity risks.

5.6 Incident Reporting: Serious incidents related to cybersecurity that may have led to or might lead to death or serious deterioration in health shall be reported to competent authorities in accordance with MDR Article 87 / IVDR Article 82. Under the proposed MDR/IVDR revision (December 2025), actively exploited vulnerabilities and severe cyber incidents shall also be reported to CSIRTs and ENISA.

articles.title: Harmonised Standards and Best Practices
articles.text:
The following harmonised and non-harmonised standards are relevant for demonstrating conformity with the cybersecurity requirements:

6.1 EN IEC 81001-5-1:2022 - Health software and health IT systems safety, effectiveness and security — Part 5-1: Security — Activities in the product life cycle. This standard is expected to be harmonised under the MDR and IVDR and provides a structured approach to cybersecurity throughout the medical device software lifecycle.

6.2 IEC 62443 series - Industrial communication networks — IT security for industrial automation and control systems. Relevant for networked medical devices, particularly IEC 62443-4-1 (secure product development lifecycle) and IEC 62443-4-2 (technical security requirements).

6.3 IEC 62304 - Medical device software — Software life cycle processes. This harmonised standard defines software development processes and should be implemented alongside IEC 81001-5-1 for comprehensive software quality and security management.

6.4 IEC TR 60601-4-5 - Medical electrical equipment — Part 4-5: Guidance and interpretation — Safety-related technical security specifications. Technical report providing guidance on cybersecurity for medical electrical equipment.

6.5 ISO 14971 - Medical devices — Application of risk management to medical devices. Risk management standard that provides the framework within which cybersecurity risk management should be integrated.

6.6 IMDRF/CYBER WG/N60 - Principles and Practices for Medical Device Cybersecurity. International harmonisation guidance providing high-level cybersecurity principles agreed by global medical device regulators.

6.7 IMDRF/CYBER WG/N73 - Principles and Practices for Software Bill of Materials for Medical Device Cybersecurity. SBOM guidance specifically for medical devices.

articles.title: Legacy Devices and Transition
articles.text: For devices already on the market, manufacturers shall assess the cybersecurity risk of existing devices and implement risk mitigation measures proportionate to the identified risks. Where the residual cybersecurity risk becomes unacceptable and cannot be mitigated through updates or compensating controls, the manufacturer shall consider end-of-life procedures, including providing sufficient notice to users and healthcare facilities, assisting with migration to replacement devices, and decommissioning guidance to prevent continued use of insecure devices.

articles.title: Role of Notified Bodies
articles.text: Notified bodies performing conformity assessment of medical devices shall evaluate the cybersecurity aspects of the technical documentation, including: adequacy of the cybersecurity risk management process, completeness and accuracy of threat modeling, appropriateness of security controls, quality and completeness of the SBOM, adequacy of post-market cybersecurity plans, and compliance with applicable harmonised standards. Notified bodies should have or have access to appropriate cybersecurity expertise to assess these aspects.
